seoTitle: How to Set Up Sub-Accounts in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Create sub-accounts in QuickBooks Online to categorize income and expenses more precisely for departments, projects, or income sources.
intro: Sub-accounts let you break broader account categories into more specific groupings, which is useful for tracking departmental budgets, project costs, or individual income sources. This guide walks through setting up sub-accounts in QuickBooks Online for clearer financial reporting.

steps.heading: Open the chart of accounts
steps.body: Navigate to QuickBooks Online's account management area where existing accounts and their structure are visible.

steps.heading: Choose the parent account
steps.body: Identify the broader account you want to nest a sub-account under, such as a general expense or income category.

steps.heading: Create the new sub-account
steps.body: Add a new account, mark it as a sub-account, and link it to the appropriate parent account.

steps.heading: Name and categorize the sub-account
steps.body: Give the sub-account a clear, specific name that reflects what it's tracking, such as a project name or department.

steps.heading: Use the sub-account in transactions
steps.body: Start assigning relevant transactions to the sub-account so it accumulates accurate data for reporting.

faq.question: How is a sub-account different from a class in QuickBooks Online?
faq.answer: Sub-accounts nest within your chart of accounts under a parent account, while classes offer a separate layer of tagging that can cut across multiple accounts.

faq.question: Can I have multiple levels of sub-accounts?
faq.answer: QuickBooks Online supports some level of nesting, though it's best to keep the structure simple enough to stay easy to manage.

faq.question: Why would a small business use sub-accounts?
faq.answer: Small businesses often use sub-accounts to track specific projects, departmental budgets, or distinct income streams under a broader category.

faq.question: Do sub-accounts show up separately on financial reports?
faq.answer: Yes, sub-accounts typically appear nested under their parent account in reports, giving you both the detailed and combined view.
